summary: My father came to the United States around the time I was born, seeking advanced music education. Our family eventually immigrated here, first living in Missouri and later moving to Minnesota for my father's doctorate. The United States became the place where my parents pursued their dreams and built a new life for us.

I was born in Sofia, Bulgaria, where my family lived before moving to the United States. My mother worked as a tour guide at the National History Museum there. Sofia represents my roots and the life we left behind when we decided to immigrate for better opportunities.

Bulgaria is where my family originally lived before coming to the United States. My mother had a stable career there as a tour guide at the National History Museum, and we had a life we intended to return to after visiting Missouri in 1998. However, my father's opportunity in Minnesota changed our plans, and we more or less permanently left Bulgaria behind.

My father came to the United States on an F1 Student Visa to attend the Kansas City Conservatory of Music for his Master's degree. This visa was the starting point of our family's journey to America and opened doors for his advanced studies and career. It was a crucial step in our immigration story.

My father attended the Kansas City Conservatory of Music on an F1 visa to earn his Master's degree in cello performance. This institution was where he honed his skills and set the foundation for his future opportunities. It was the first major milestone in his musical and academic journey in the United States.

In 1998, my mother and I traveled to Missouri on visiting visas to join my father, and we initially planned to return to Bulgaria. We lived there for about a year, during which my twin sisters were born. Missouri was our first home in the United States and the place where our family's future started to change.

After a professor in Minnesota offered my father a chance to pursue a Doctorate in cello performance for free, our family moved there permanently. Minnesota became our new home where my parents both pursued higher education at the University of Minnesota. Living there shaped much of my upbringing and our family's American experience.

My father pursued his Doctorate in cello performance at the University of Minnesota, thanks to a generous professor's offer. My mother also completed her master's in history there by transferring her credits from Bulgaria. We lived in housing affiliated with the university, making it central to our family's life and education.

Our family lived in the Commonwealth Terrace Cooperative, a housing program for families attending the University of Minnesota. It was a diverse community with many immigrant families, which enriched my childhood experience. This cooperative provided us with a supportive environment during our studies and transition to life in America.

Before moving to the United States, my mother worked as a tour guide at the National History Museum in Sofia, Bulgaria. She had a stable and promising career there, which made the decision to immigrate even more courageous. Her background at the museum reflects the life and opportunities we left behind to pursue new ones here.
